coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots-doped dummy molecularly imprinted polymer monolithic column for selective enrichment and analysis of aflatoxin B1 in peanut
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Guohuan
Zhai, Haiyun
Huang, Lu
Tan, Xuecai
Zhou, Qing
Yu, Xiao
Lin, Haidan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel method was proposed to sensitively determine aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in peanut sample by using a carbon quantum dots-coated dummy molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs-DMIP) monolithic column for pretreatment coupled with high performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD). The CDs-DMIP monolithic column was prepared by in-situ polymerization in a water bath using 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin as dummy template molecule. The CDs-DMIP monolithic column was applied to determine AFB1 by HPLC-FLD. Satisfactory linearity was obtained over 0.5–2000ngmL−1, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9999. The recoveries of AFB1 in peanut sample ranged from 79.5% to 91.2%, and the intraday and interday relative standard deviation ranged from 1.2% to 4.9%. Limit of detection (S/N=3) and limit of quantitation (S/N=10) were 0.118ngmL−1 and 0.393ngmL−1, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the enrichment factor was over 71-fold. AFB1 in peanut sample and even some other samples could be sensitively determined by CDs-DMIP-HPLC-FLD method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin B1
Molecularly imprinted monolithic columns
Carbon quantum dots
High performance liquid chromatography

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and morphology of CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column
Capacity of CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column
Selection of washing solvent and eluent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of (a) AEAPMS, (b) CDs and (c) CDs-DMIP.
The chemical structure of AFB1, DMC and ST.

SEM images of (a) the CDs-DMIP column synthesized with MAA, (b) the column prepared with AM, (c) the column polymerized at 365nm UV lamp, (d) the magnification is 1000 folds for a, (e) the magnification is 5000 folds for a, (f) the DMIP column.
Breakthrough curves of AFB1 and its structural analogues on different columns.

Illustrative chromatograms for the sample impurities removal and enrichment: (a)peanut sample after loaded onto CDs-DMIP column; (b) 0.3ngmL−1AFB1 peanut sample before loaded onto CDs-DMIP column; (c) 0.3ngmL−1 AFB1 peanut sample after loaded onto CDs-DMIP column.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The column was dense, no solution can pass through, and no data were obtained.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of AFB1 spiked in peanut sample.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots-doped dummy molecularly imprinted polymer monolithic column for selective enrichment and analysis of aflatoxin B1 in peanut

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University for Nationalities, Key Laboratory of Guangxi Colleges and Universities for Food Safety and Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Nanning, 530008,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Guangxi Colleges and Universities for Food Safety and Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Institute of Veterinary Drug and Feed Inspection, Guangzhou, 510230, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Institute of Veterinary Drug and Feed Inspection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel method was proposed to sensitively determine aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in peanut sample by using a carbon quantum dots-coated dummy molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs-DMIP) monolithic column for pretreatment coupled with high performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD). The CDs-DMIP monolithic column was prepared by in-situ polymerization in a water bath using 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin as dummy template molecule. The CDs-DMIP monolithic column was applied to determine AFB1 by HPLC-FLD. Satisfactory linearity was obtained over 0.5–2000ngmL−1, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9999. The recoveries of AFB1 in peanut sample ranged from 79.5% to 91.2%, and the intraday and interday relative standard deviation ranged from 1.2% to 4.9%. Limit of detection (S/N=3) and limit of quantitation (S/N=10) were 0.118ngmL−1 and 0.393ngmL−1, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the enrichment factor was over 71-fold. AFB1 in peanut sample and even some other samples could be sensitively determined by CDs-DMIP-HPLC-FLD method.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is one of the most important aflatoxins with acute toxicity. More than 18 kinds of aflatoxin isomers have been found so far, mainly including AFB1, B2, G1, G2, M1 and M2 [1] (Fig. S1). Aflatoxin is a secondary metabolite mostly produced by Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus, existing in soil, animals plants and various nuts, especially peanuts, corns, rices, soybeans, wheats and so on [2]. AFB1 has severe carcinogenic toxicity, which is 10 times that of potassium cyanide, 68 times that of arsenic and 75 times that of dimethyl nitrosamine in inducing hepatocellular carcinoma. Due to the acute toxicity of AFB1 to humans, it was recognized as group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) in 1993 and one of the world's three strongest carcinogens. In view of the great dangers of AFB1, many countries and organizations have set strict legal limits. The World Health Organization, the Codex Alimentarius Commission, the European Union (EU), the United States and Japan stipulated that the total level of aflatoxins (B1+B2+G1+G2) in food should be lower than 15μgkg−1.EU even stipulated an AFB1 limit of 2.0μgkg−1 in food, and Japan forbade the existence of AFB1 in food. Therefore, the detection of AFB1 is of great significance. At present, AFB1 is commonly detected by thin layer chromatography (TLC) [3], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, (LC–MS) [7], and electrochemical sensors [8]. However, the content of AFB1 in actual sample is very low, so an appropriate pretreatment method must be proposed to obtain the accurate results.

Molecularly imprinted monolithic column can be used for sample pretreatment of AFB1 owing to a predetermined specificity toward a given analyte. These columns are prepared in the presence of functional monomers, cross-linkers and molecular templates [9]. The materials inside the monolith are molecular imprinted polymers (MIP), providing cavities corresponding to template molecules leading to high specificity. Thus, MIP is suitable for the analyzing trace substances in complex matrix samples. Up to now, researchers have endeavored to prepare MIP for AFB1 detection, and attracted more attention [10–12].

Since preparing MIP for AFB1 requires template molecule, considerable AFB1 is inevitably consumed. Considering the high toxicity and cost of AFB1, 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin (DMC), a structural analog of AFB1, was used as an alternative template to prepare MIP. Wyszomirski et al. [13] used DMC as an alternative template to synthesize MIP, and found that the interaction between DMC and methacrylic acid (MAA) or allylamine was similar to that of AFB1. This method has high selectivity and low costs, also avoiding “template leakage” caused by inefficient template elution and increasing the solubility of template. As a result, molecular imprinting has been widely used in sample pretreatment based on the advantages of alternative template [14–16], but it has rarely been used to detect mycotoxins, especially aflatoxins [17].

To improve the performance of molecularly imprinted monolithic column and the enrichment effect of MIP on target molecule, restricted-access media and nanomaterials have gradually been employed to prepare MIP [18–20]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as emerging quantum-sized carbon nanomaterials, have been applied to molecular imprinting due to green synthesis, photoluminescence, easy functionalization and low toxicity [21,22]. Nevertheless, CDs have seldom been used in the molecular imprinting of aflatoxin, with only a few studies on other mycotoxins [16].

In this work, DMC was used as a dummy template for AFB1, MAA as functional monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA) as cross-linker and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) as initiator, CDs were doped in MIP and molecularly imprinted monolithic column was prepared by in-situ polymerization. With high specificity, MIP bound CDs with large specific surface area, facilitating the separation and enrichment of AFB1. The performances of the CDs-DMIP column were evaluated by column capacity, recovery, precision, enrichment effect, and the morphology and structure of monolithic column were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, finally, the monolithic column was used for the analysis of AFB1 in food sample.

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), MAA, EDMA, AIBN, acrylamide (AM), anhydrous citric acid, AFB1, DMC and staphylococcal (ST) were all purchased from Aladdin industrial corporation (Shanghai, China). HPLC-grade methanol was purchased from Merck Chemicals (Germany). All analytical reagent grade chemical reagents used in this study were from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Factory. Glass capillary (10cm long, 0.5mm, i.d.) was supplied by West China Medical University Instrument Factory (China). Samples were purchased from a local market. The distilled water was obtained from Watsons, and all samples were dissolved and filtered through 0.45μm nylon cylinder membrane filter (Tianjin Jinteng Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd.) before HPLC analysis.

Determination of CDs was completed on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra were acquired on a PerkinElmer Spectrum100 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (America). The UV absorption curve of CDs was completed on UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) experiments were performed on Shimadzu LC-20A high performance liquid chromatography (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with Shimadzu RF-20AXL FLD (Shimadzu, Japan). The micro injection pump (Longer Pump Company) was used to inject samples to monolithic column. The surface characteristics of the DMIP and NIP were investigated by a ZEISS MERLIN scanning electron microscope (Germany).

The synthetic method of highly luminescent CDs was optimized based on the one-step reaction reported by Wang et al. [23]. Briefly, 10mL of AEAPMS was added to a 100mL three-neck flask purged with nitrogen for 5min, heated to 240°C, and rapidly added 0.5g anhydrous citric acid under vigorous stirring, the mixture was reacted for 5min, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The CDs were precipitated three times with petroleum ether, and the resulting precipitate (reddish brown oily liquid) was re-dissolved in methanol for subsequent experiments.

Before CDs-DMIP was synthesized, capillaries were soaked with 1molL−1 NaOH solution for 1h, with deionized water for 10min and with 1molL−1 HCl solution for 1h. Afterwards, the capillaries were cleaned with deionized water until the eluate was neutral. After capillaries drying, the capillaries were treated with 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane-methanol (1:1, v/v) for 12h in a 40°C water-bath, and then rinsed with methanol. The silanized capillaries were used to synthesize CDs-DMIP column. Briefly, dummy template DMC (0.025mmol) and functional monomer MAA (0.25mmol) were dissolved in a mixture of toluene (0.1mL) and methanol (0.4mL), and then added CDs (0.3mL). After 5min of ultrasonication, cross-linker EDMA (1mmol) and initiator AIBN (0.05mmol) were added. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 10min and purged with nitrogen for 10min. The mixture was siphoned by capillary columns, then both ends sealed by hot melt adhesive before polymerization. The reaction was initiated in water bath at 55°C for 6h. After polymerization, the CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary columns were washed with methanol at a flow rate of 0.02mLmin−1 to remove any unreacted reagents and dummy templates by microinjection pump. The dummy template molecules were removed with methanol-acetic acid (9:1, v/v) until no DMC was determined by HPLC-FLD. CDs-coated NIP (CDs-NIP) was also prepared using the same procedure, only without the addition of DMC, and NIP was neither DMC nor CDs.

CDs-DMIP column was used to determine AFB1 in peanut sample purchased from a local market. Peanut sample (25g) was pulverized, 5g of which was added into a 100mL flask, and then added 25mL of methanol-water (8:2, v/v) mixture, shaked for 30min, centrifuged for 5min at 3000rpm, the extraction was then filtered through a 0.45μm filter for further analysis. The concentrations of spiked samples were 10ngmL−1, 50ngmL−1 and 100ngmL−1, respectively.

The synthetic columns were fixed to the syringe needles with glue to remove the templates and unreacted reagents by micro-injection pump at a flow rate of 0.01mLmin−1. Before injection, the CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column was activated with 80μL methanol and 80μL water. The injected CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column was washed with 40μL toluene and eluted with 100μL methanol-acetic acid (9:1, v/v) at the same flow rate, respectively. The eluent was collected and evaporated under nitrogen to dryness, and then reconstituted with 0.1mL of methanol for further HPLC detection.

Shimadzu LC-20A HPLC system (Shimadzu, Japan) configured with one LC-20ATVP pump, one automatic sampler, and one Shimadzu RF-20AXL fluorescence detection was used for HPLC analysis. Ecosil-EPS-C18 column (250 mm×4.6mm, 5μm particle size) was used for separation, and the mobile phase was methanol-water (60:40, v/v) with a flow rate of 1.0mLmin−1. The injection volume was 20μL, and the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were set as 360nm and 440nm, respectively.

CDs were synthesized by an efficient one-step reaction, i.e. the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid in AEAPMS, with the formation of gas and water vapors [23]. The surface passivation reaction of the CDs was accomplished by the amino group of AEAPMS with the carboxyl group produced by pyrolysis, and the organosilane-functionalized CDs were highly soluble in most organic solvents with strong blue light (Fig. S2). The acylation reaction of surface passivation was confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1b, the strong and characteristic peaks at 1650cm−1 and 1565cm−1 correspond to amide I and II vibrations, respectively, unlike the spectrum of pure AEAPMS (Fig. 1a). The vibration of CONR confirms successful acylation reaction of CDs. Additionally, there are peaks at 2930cm−1 and 2880cm−1 for CH, 3398cm−1 for COH, 1480cm−1 for CO, 1079cm−1 for COSi and 800cm−1 for SiO (Fig. 1b), further confirming that CDs had been successfully synthesized. There is an obvious absorption peak centered at 355nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. S3). CDs emit strong blue fluorescence (FL) at 460nm when excited at 340–400nm (Fig. S3a–f). The optimum excitation wavelength of CDs was 365nm. With increasing excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength bathochromically shifts, accompanied by decreased FL intensity. Contrarily, the wavelength hypsochromically shifts and FL intensity also drops. Hu et al. and others [24,25] have attributed variations of the FL intensity and emission wavelength of CDs along with excitation wavelength to the quantum effect of CDs and surface defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and morphology of CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column
ce:section.ce:para: Porogen is crucial to the preparation of CDs-DMIP column, and the porogenic solvent must be able to dissolve the template and functional monomer. In this study, toluene, methanol, acetonitrile, methanol-toluene and methanol-chloroform-dodecanol were tested to select the appropriate porogen. Methanol-toluene functioned the best. Template, MAA and EDMA were well dissolved in methanol, and toluene formed uniform cavities. Compared with methanol-chloroform-dodecanol, the CDs-DMIP column prepared by methanol-toluene was more beneficial to template elution. Moreover, the influence of molar ratio of template, monomer and cross-linker (T:M:C) on the CDs-DMIP column performance was tested. When T:M:C was 1:10:40, the column showed the highest mechanical strength and permeability, with a good recovery (Table 1 ). The CDs-DMIP column became more compact with increasing ratio of C (EDMA) or decreasing ratio of M (MAA). Otherwise, the monolithic column loosened.

The influence of CDs on the column was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3d and f, the CDs-DMIP column provides more uniform pores for the analytes than the DMIP column does, thereby augmenting the adsorption capacity of the column and reducing its pressure. We first found that the template elution process of CDs-DMIP was easier than that of DMIP (Fig. S5), probably due to the porous structure of the former.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacity of CDs-DMIP monolithic capillary column
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of CDs-DMIP column was tested by plotting breakthrough curves. In brief, 5ngmL−1 AFB1 standard solution was loaded onto a 7cm CDs-DMIP column (DMIP weight, 2.92mg) and another CDs-NIP column (NIP weight, 2.90mg) of the same length at a flow rate of 0.01mLmin−1, respectively, and the effluent was collected and detected by HPLC. The breakthrough curves of CDs-DMIP and CDs-NIP columns were plotted based on injection volume and sample content. As shown in Fig. 4 , there were no analytes in the beginning, and the concentration of AFB1 then gradually increases until the loading concentration. The capacities of CDs-DMIP for AFB1 (CDs-DMIP-AFB1) and CDs-NIP for AFB1 (CDs-NIP-AFB1) were then calculated as 0.442ngmg−1 and 0.0917ngmg−1, respectively. Accordingly, CDs-DMIP exhibited a higher affinity to AFB1 than CDs-NIP did.

To further compare the specificity of CDs-DMIP prepared by alternative template to MDC and AFB1, 5ngmL−1 DMC standard solution was loaded onto a 7cm CDs-DMIP column (DMIP weight, 2.92mg), and the effluent was collected for HPLC analysis. The capacity of CDs-DMIP for DMC (CDs-DMIP-DMC) was calculated as 0.478ngmg−1, and its specificity to MDC and AFB1 were similar (Fig. 4), indicating that CDs-DMIP prepared by dummy template was also highly selective to AFB1.

In addition, to confirm that CDs elevated the capacity of the DMIP column, 5ngmL−1 AFB1 standard solution was loaded onto a 7cm DMIP column (DMIP weight, 2.92mg) following the same steps, and the capacity of MIP was calculated as 0.263ngmg−1. The CDs-DMIP column showed higher capacity than that of the DMIP column, suggesting that CDs effectively increased the column capacity.

For the sake of proving the specificity of CDs-DMIP to AFB1, ST (Fig. 2), a structural analogue of AFB1, was tested. Briefly, 5ngmL−1 ST standard solution was loaded onto a 7cm CDs-DMIP column (DMIP weight, 2.92mg), and the effluent was collected and analyzed by HPLC. As shown in Fig. 4, the capacity of ST is 0.163ngmg−1, indicating that CDs-DMIP had a higher specificity to AFB1 than ST because the structure of AFB1 was more similar to that of DMC.

The adsorption of AFB1 on CDs-DMIP and CDs-NIP was performed using batch experiment. We conducted three batch experiments with three columns in each batch, the results were shown in Table S1 and the sorption capacity (Qe) for CDs-DMIP was calculated as 0.452ngmg−1, obviously superior to that of predecessors [26]. Based on the parameters of Qe, the imprinting factor (IF) was calculated as 4.9 for CDs-DMIP of AFB1 using the following equation:

IF=QMIP/QNIP (1)where QMIP is adsorption capacity of CDs-DMIP and QNIP is the adsorption capacity of CDs-NIP, as shown in Table S1. In addition, the columns are effective for multiple uses. Fig. S6 showed no significant drop in column capacity, even though they have been used fifteen times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of washing solvent and eluent
ce:section.ce:para: Fat-soluble impurities were easily absorbed by columns when extracted AFB1 from the actual sample. Low polarity solvents, such as toluene and chloroform, have generally been used as washing solvents to remove impurities. Three solvents were investigated herein. Toluene was able to effectively remove impurities, with high recovery. Ether was capable of impurity removal, with low recovery though. Chloroform loosened the CDs-DMIP column, washing out some polymers. As a result, toluene was selected as the washing solvent. The choice of eluent was carried out in four different mixtures, as shown in Table 2 . When 100ngmL−1 AFB1 standard solution was loaded onto the CDs-DMIP column and eluted with 80μL of methanol-acetic acid (9:1, v/v), the recovery of AFB1 reached 96.6%. The effect of flow rate was investigated using CDs-DMIP columns. 0.5mL of AFB1 standard solution (25ngmL−1) was respectively loaded onto the CDs-DMIP columns at different flow rate, the results showed that the sorption capacity decreased when the flow rate was above 0.01mLmin−1.

Calibration curves were plotted based on the concentration and peak areas. Under the optimized conditions, the linear equation was A=10015C-18636 over 0.5–2000ngmL−1, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9999. The precision was carried out with three different concentrations of spiked samples (Table 3 ). The recoveries ranged from 79.5% to 91.2%, and both the interday and intraday relative standard deviations were lower than 5%. When the concentration was 10ngmL−1, the recovery was the best. The limit of detection (LOD, S/N=3) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ, S/N=10) were calculated as 0.118ngmL−1 and 0.393ngmL−1, respectively. Compared with traditional SPE-HPLC [27] (3.0ngg−1) and UV–vis spectrophotometry [28] (23.6μgL−1), this method was more sensitive. The feasibility of the developed method was validated using statutory method (GB 5009.22-2016). These two methods were used to determine the same actual sample, the results showed that the developed method had good repeatability and detection limit (Table S2), and there was no significant difference between the method and the statutory method.

The enrichment of CDs-DMIP column was evaluated with spiked peanut sample. Briefly, 5mL of spiked peanut sample (0.3ngmL−1 AFB1) was all injected into the CDs-DMIP column, washed with toluene and eluted with methanol-acetic acid (9:1, v/v), and the eluent was analyzed by HPLC-FLD (Fig. 5 c). Fig. 5a presented the chromatogram of peanut sample without AFB1 after being injected into the CDs-DMIP column, and Fig. 5b showed that of peanut sample spiked with 0.3ngmL−1 AFB1 before CDs-DMIP column extraction. As shown in Fig. 5c, there was an obviously AFB1 peak, accompanied by significantly decreased impurity peaks, the sample concentration was calculated by peak area and standard curve, and the enrichment factor was over 71-fold. Hence, CDs-DMIP column raised the sensitivity of the proposed method through effective enrichment and impurity removal. For the enrichment of AFB1 in other samples such as food samples barley, maize and traditional Chinese medicine samples barley and nutmeg, this method also showed satisfactory results. The contents of AFB1 in the samples (Table S3) were lower than the permissible level (2.0μgkg−1) established by European Commission (EC), and no AFB1 was detected in maize sample.

A novel CDs-DMIP monolithic column was prepared by dummy template, exhibiting high specificity and good enrichment for AFB1 with outstanding impurity removal effect. In addition, the CDs-DMIP column could be conveniently synthesized by in-situ polymerization with conventional reagents. CDs effectively improved the capacity and sensitivity of this column. A feasible and sensitive approach for determining AFB1 in peanut sample was successfully developed by using the CDs-DMIP column as separation and enrichment technology coupled with HPLC-FLD. LOD was 0.118ngmL−1, the linear range was from 0.5 to 2000ngmL−1 (r=0.9999), and the enrichment factor was over 71-fold. This method had higher sensitivity and recovery for sample analysis than those of other strategies. In summary, it is feasible to detect AFB1 in peanut sample using the CDs-DMIP column, giving excellent results even in other food samples and traditional Chinese medicine samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant Nos. 21005021 and 21375152), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2016A030313740) and Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Project (No. 2013B040402010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2017.11.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An empirical evaluation of three vibrational spectroscopic methods for detection of aflatoxins in maize
Effect of pressure cooking on aflatoxin B1 in rice
Assessment of aflatoxin B1 in livestock feed and feed ingredients by high-performance thin layer chromatography
Quality evaluation of five commercial enzyme linked immunosorbent assay kits for detecting aflatoxin B1 in feedstuffs
Determination of aflatoxins B1, B2 G1, and G2 in olive oil, peanut oil, and sesame oil
Screening of aflatoxins in feed samples using a flow system coupled to capillary electrophoresis
Micro-solid phase extraction with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry for the determination of aflatoxins in coffee and malt beverage
Electrochemical sensor for determination of aflatoxin B1 based on multiwalled carbon nanotubes-supported Au/Pt bimetallic nanoparticles
Molecularly imprinted solid phase extraction coupled to high performance liquid chromatography for determination of aflatoxin M1 and B1 in foods and feeds
Aflatoxin B1 detection using a highly-sensitive molecularly-imprinted electrochemical sensor based on an electropolymerized metal organic framework
Monolithic molecularly imprinted polymeric capillary columns for isolation of aflatoxins
Sensor system based on molecular-imprinted polymer membranes for the selective recognition of aflatoxin B1
Molecular modelling of a template subst-itute and monomers used in molecular imprinting for aflatoxin B1 micro-HPLC analysis
Molecularly imprinted polymers with a streamlined mimic for zearalenone analysis
Molecularly imprinted polymers with synthetic dummy template for simultaneously selective removal and enrichment of ginkgolic acids from Ginkgo biloba L. leaves extracts
One-pot synthesis of carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer for specific recognition of sterigmatocystin in grains
Molecularly imprinted polymer solid-phase extraction for detection of zearalenone in cereal sample extracts
Restricted access media-molecularly imprinted polymer for propranolol and its application to direct injection analysis of beta-blockers in biological fluids
Chip-based molecularly imprinted monolithic capillary array columns coated GO/SiO2 for selective extraction and sensitive determination of rhodamine B in chili powder
Molecularly imprinted coated graphene oxide solid-phase extraction monolithic capillary column for selective extraction and sensitive determination of phloxine B in coffee bean
Selective fluorescent sensing of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers
Selective determination of dimethoate via fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and a dye-doped molecularly imprinted polymer
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Multiclonal plastic antibodies for selective aflatoxin extraction from food samples
Matrix-free analysis of aflatoxins in pistachio nuts using parallel factor modeling of liquid chromatography diode-array detection data

Application of response surface methodology for air assisted-dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction of deoxynivalenol in rice samples prior to HPLC-DAD analysis and comparison with solid phase extraction cleanup
